text: This video is going to demonstrate robotic lateral pelvic lymph node dissection in a case of rectal cancer. Our patient is a fifty six year old gentleman with complaints of bleeding perirectum who was diagnosed to have non-metastatic adenocarcinoma of the lower third rectum. MRI pelvis showed a rectal lesion three centimeters from the anal verge along with multiple perirectal nodes and left internal lydia node. He was treated with long-course concurrent chemoradiotherapy followed by robotic abdominal pinional resection with left lateral pelvic lymph node dissection as the post-treatment MRI showed only partial response of the primary with persistent left internal iliac lymph nodes.

This video is going to demonstrate robotic lateral pelvic lymph node dissection in a case of rectal cancer. Our patient is a fifty six year old gentleman with complaints of bleeding perirectum who was diagnosed to have non-metastatic adenocarcinoma of the lower third rectum. MRI pelvis showed a rectal lesion three centimeters from the anal verge along with multiple perirectal nodes and left internal lydia node. He was treated with long-course concurrent chemoradiotherapy followed by robotic abdominal pinional resection with left lateral pelvic lymph node dissection as the post-treatment MRI showed only partial response of the primary with persistent left internal iliac lymph nodes.

The surgeon uses a five-port approach for the robotic procedure. The pelvic lymph node dissection begins after mobilizing the rectum and completing total mesorectal excision. The ureter is identified along its pelvic course, and the peritoneum over the pelvic portion of the ureter is incised. The ureter is then mobilized from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery to the ureterovesical junction, gently pushing it medially.

The surgeon uses a standard five-port approach for robotic pelvic lymph node dissection. After mobilizing the rectum and completing total mesorectal excision, the ureter is identified along its pelvic course. The peritoneum over the pelvic portion of the ureter is incised, and the ureter is mobilized from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery to the ureterovesical junction, gently pushing it medially.

The surgeon completes the medialization of the ureter and proceeds to skeletonize the external iliac vessels. The external iliac artery is identified and dissected anteriorly and medially, then rolled laterally to expose the external iliac vein. The dissection progresses either radially to caudally or vice versa, revealing the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. Care is taken to avoid injuring the ureter as it crosses from lateral to medial at this junction.

After completing the medialization of the ureter, the surgeon identifies and skeletonizes the external iliac artery, dissecting it anteriorly and medially. The artery is then rolled laterally to expose the external iliac vein. The dissection proceeds either radially to caudally or vice versa, with the bifurcation of the common iliac artery becoming visible. Care is taken to avoid injuring the ureter as it crosses from lateral to medial at this junction.

The surgeon skeletonizes the internal iliac artery by dissecting fibrofiber tissue around it in a cradle to caudal fashion. The dissection then proceeds towards the bladder surface, exposing the obliterated umbilical artery, which divides the paravisical space into medial and lateral parts. The lateral pelvic lymph node dissection template is defined, extending laterally to the pelvic wall, medially to the bladder and obliterated umbilical artery, proximally from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, distally to the D-sopharynx ilia vein, and posteriorly to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon skeletonizes the internal iliac artery in a cradle to caudal fashion, meticulously dissecting fibrofiber tissue surrounding it. The dissection progresses towards the bladder surface, exposing the obliterated umbilical artery, which divides the paravisical space into medial and lateral sections. The lateral pelvic lymph node dissection template is defined, extending laterally to the pelvic wall, medially to the bladder and obliterated umbilical artery, proximally from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, distally to the D-sopharynx ilia vein, and posteriorly to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon continues dissection within the established template, pulling the bladder wall laterally to clear fibro-fatty tissue from the lateral paravisical space. The obturator nerve is exposed as it enters the obturator canal, and fibrofatty tissue anterior to the nerve is dissected out. The dissection extends posterior to the obturator nerve, clearing fibrofatty tissue up to the pelvic floor for radical oncological clearance.

The surgeon continues dissection within the established template, pulling the bladder wall laterally and clearing fibro fatty tissue from the lateral paravisical space. The obturator nerve is exposed as it enters the obturator canal, and the fibrofatty tissue anterior to the nerve is dissected out. The dissection extends posterior to the obturator nerve, removing all fibrofatty tissue up to the pelvic floor for complete oncological clearance.

The surgeon carefully dissects the branches of the anterior division of the internal iliac artery using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques. The obliterated umbilical artery, bladder wall, superior vesicular artery, anterior division of the internal iliac artery, obturator vessels, and pelvic floor are identified. The specimen is extracted using an endobag, concluding the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the branches of the anterior division of the internal iliac artery using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques. The obliterated umbilical artery, bladder wall, superior vesicular artery, anterior division of the internal iliac artery, obturator vessels, and pelvic floor are identified. The specimen is extracted using an endobag, marking the completion of the procedure.

The total duration of the pelvic lymphodiscretion was ninety-five minutes and there was only minimal blood loss. The patient had no peri-morbidity and the final histopathology report showed six nodes dissected, all negative for tumor.
